title: JMAP Chat: local user-agent server bridging Agora relay
description:
Implement JMAP Chat support using the mailbox-per-user topology from draft-atwood-jmap-chat-00 (https://github.com/MarkAtwood/ideas/blob/main/draft-atwood-jmap-chat-00.md).

The Agora relay is a stateless message router and cannot be the JMAP server. Instead, the user agent (agora-cli) runs a local JMAP server on localhost. State lives on the user's machine. The relay is unchanged.

Architecture:

  [JMAP client]
        ↕ JMAP over HTTP (localhost)
  [agora-cli — local JMAP server + local SQLite message store]
        ↕ Agora WebSocket protocol (sealed_broadcast / sealed_deliver)
  [Agora relay — unchanged, stateless]

Inbound: sealed_deliver → agent decrypts → inserts into local JMAP Message store → EventSource push to connected JMAP clients.
Outbound: JMAP Message/set → agent encrypts → sealed_broadcast to relay.

The user's did:key IS their JMAP account identifier. E2EE is transparent to the JMAP client — the agent seals/unseals, the client sees plaintext.

Work areas:

1. Local HTTP listener: JMAP Core (RFC 8620) session endpoint, batch request handler, EventSource push.
2. Local SQLite schema: Messages, Chats, Contacts (did:key → JMAP Contact), Reactions, Threads, ReadState, StateTokens.
3. JMAP Chat types to implement: Chat, Message, Contact, ChatMember, Reaction, MessageRevision, Mention, Attachment (subset — not all 16 types needed for MVP).
4. Bridge loop: Agora WS receive → decrypt → JMAP store insert → push state change.
5. Bridge loop: JMAP Message/set → encrypt → sealed_broadcast.
6. Reactions/edits/threads: define a small application-layer envelope schema on top of sealed payloads so peer agents can distinguish new messages from metadata updates.
7. Multi-device: explicitly out of scope for MVP. Document the tradeoff (same as Signal — single device or manual backup).

Known hard parts:
- JMAP state token machinery (/changes methods, state strings) is non-trivial to implement correctly.
- Reactions and edits require a shared envelope schema that all peer agents agree on — this needs to be specced before implemented.
- New installs have no history (accepted tradeoff for now).
design: Mailbox-per-user topology (explicitly described in draft-atwood-jmap-chat-00). The relay's statelessness is preserved and becomes a privacy feature — the relay is just a sealed bus. Each user's agent is their own JMAP server. did:key maps cleanly as the JMAP account identifier. No relay changes required.
notes: JMAP Chat draft lives at https://github.com/MarkAtwood/ideas/blob/main/draft-atwood-jmap-chat-00.md — 16 data types defined, but MVP needs only the core subset. Federation (Peer/* methods) is explicitly out of scope — the relay handles routing.
